title: How much, how many
rule: Partitive for amounts: after numbers, for mass nouns, and in 'there is/are'
summary: The other half of the partitive has nothing to do with finishing things - it's about quantity. Any number above one puts the thing that follows into the partitive SINGULAR (kaks kahvia, not kaks kahvit), stuff you can't count sits in the partitive by default (vettä, aikaa), and 'there is / there are' takes it too. Get this wrong and you sound like you're counting in a foreign grammar; get it right and prices, orders and shopping all come out naturally.

items.note: After any number but one, Finnish uses the partitive SINGULAR - kaks kahvia, never a plural. English pluralises, Finnish does the opposite.
euroa → euroo in speech. This is why prices sound the way they do: 'seittemän kakskyt' - the euro itself often gets dropped once the number is clear.
kolme omenaa - singular partitive again, even though there are three. 'Kolme omenat' is the mistake nearly every learner makes once.

items.prompt: Make it TWO - watch the ending on this one
items.note: olut takes -ta, so kaks olutta. The number rule doesn't change which partitive ending a word uses - it only decides that you need one.

items.prompt: Ask for water - you can't count it
items.note: vesi → vettä, an everyday irregular worth knowing cold. Mass nouns - water, milk, coffee, money, time - live in the partitive unless you mean one specific whole portion.

items.prompt: Say you have time, not a specific hour
items.note: aika → aikaa. 'Mul on aika' would mean an appointment - the partitive is what turns it into the uncountable 'time'.

items.prompt: Say there are people there, not one person
items.from_en: There's a person in the shop.
items.to_en: There are people in the shop.
items.note: An indefinite number of things uses the PLURAL partitive: ihmisiä, spoken ihmisii. Note the verb stays singular - 'on', never 'ovat'. That trips up speakers of every other European language.

items.prompt: Say there are books on the table
items.from_en: There's a book on the table.
items.to_en: There are books on the table.
items.note: kirja → kirjoja in the plural partitive. Still 'on', still singular verb.

items.prompt: Say you'd like a bit of it
items.to_en: I'll take a bit of bun.
items.note: Quantity words - vähän, paljon, vähän lisää - all pull the partitive after them, exactly like numbers do.

items.prompt: Say there's a lot of it
items.to_en: I have a lot of work.
items.note: työ → työtä (the -tä form, harmonising with the front vowel ö). paljon takes the partitive just like a number does.
